message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

<command-name>/effort</command-name>
            <command-message>effort</command-message>
            <command-args>high</command-args>
<local-command-stdout>Set effort level to high (saved as your default for new sessions): Comprehensive implementation with extensive testing and documentation</local-command-stdout>

<local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

<command-name>/effort</command-name>
            <command-message>effort</command-message>
            <command-args></command-args>
<local-command-stdout>Set effort level to high (saved as your default for new sessions): Comprehensive implementation with extensive testing and documentation</local-command-stdout>

A goal of this project is to be able to see all the bot session conversations,
not just the current one, and also to see the change associated with a particular
prompt or set of prompts. To that end the next think I'd like to is implement
a command to display a .claude/xxx.jsonl file. I asked web claude and they
suggested https://github.com/alfredvc/cct as a library that might work. I've
cloned it to ../clones/cct/.

I then installed the example check_one and ran:
```
wink@3900x 26-07-17T16:03:44.574Z:~/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1 (main+1)
$ check_one .claude/4f5d498c-da5b-40ca-820e-a89676a58d79.jsonl
Transcript: .claude/4f5d498c-da5b-40ca-820e-a89676a58d79.jsonl
  Lines:     485
  OK:        134
  Parse err: 0
  RT diff:   351

  ── Roundtrip diffs ───────────────────────────────────────
    line    4  type=user
      missing in output:  promptSource = "typed"
    line    5  type=attachment
      missing in output:  attachment.pendingMcpServers = []
    line    8  type=attachment
      missing in output:  attachment.names = ["deep-research","dataviz","artifact-design","artifact-capabilities","update-config","keybindings-help","verify","code-review","simplify","fewer-permission-prompts","loop","schedule","claude-api","claude-in-chrome","run","init","review","security-review"]
    line   10  type=assistant
..
```

So the initial observation is that cct is out-of-date which is known as Anthropic
evolves the transcript information in the bot repo continuously. So thoughts and
maybe there are more updated crates?

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- claude-in-chrome: Automates your Chrome browser to interact with web pages - clicking elements, filling forms, capturing screenshots, reading console logs, and navigating sites. Opens pages in new tabs within your existing Chrome session. Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. Always invoke BEFORE attempting to use any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tools.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.description: List cct workspace crates and read README intro

content.content:
claude-code-transcripts claude-code-transcripts-ingest

cct — Claude Code Transcripts

**Your Claude Code transcripts as SQL.** `cct` ingests every transcript under `~/.claude/projects` into a local DuckDB. Skills tell Claude how to investigate it — so you can ask questions about your own usage in plain English and get answers backed by your real history, not generic advice. The primitive is the database. The skills are playbooks on top. Cost optimization is one playbook; you can write your own. ## Install ### 1. `cct` binary ```bash curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Alfredvc/cct/main/install.sh | sh ``` Downloads the latest prebuilt `cct` binary into `~/.local/bin`. Override with `CCT_INSTALL_DIR=/some/dir` or pin a version with `CCT_VERSION=v0.2.0`. Source: [`crates/claude-code-transcripts-ingest/`](crates/claude-code-transcripts-ingest/). After install, upgrade in place with `cct update` (or `cct update --version v0.2.0` to pin). `cct` checks GitHub for a newer release once every 24 hours in the background and prints a one-line banner on stderr when one is available. Set `CCT_NO_UPDATE_CHECK=1` (or `CI=true`) to disable. Cache lives at `~/.cache/cct/update_check.json`. ### 2. Skills ```bash npx skills add alfredvc/cct ``` Installs the agent skills below into Claude Code. ### 3. DuckDB CLI Skills query the DB via the `duckdb` CLI. Install from [duckdb.org](https://duckdb.org/install/?platform=macos&environment=cli) or:

content.content:
[package]
name = "weavr"
version = "1.2.0"
edition = "2021"
rust-version = "1.80"
description = "Claude Code transcript exporter — beautiful, self-contained HTML and Markdown"
license = "MIT"
repository = "https://github.com/deepakkumardewani/weavr"
readme = "README.md"
keywords = ["claude-code", "transcript", "exporter", "html", "markdown"]
categories = ["command-line-utilities", "development-tools", "text-processing"]
homepage = "https://github.com/deepakkumardewani/weavr"
# Keep the published crate lean — ship only what's needed to build + run.
# Everything below is dev/internal tooling not required by crate consumers.
exclude = [
    "agent_docs/",
    "tests/",
    "screenshots/",
    "scripts/",
    ".github/",
    "justfile",
    "install.sh",
    "clippy.toml",
    "rustfmt.toml",
    "CLAUDE.md",
    ".impeccable.md",
]

[dependencies]
# Phase 1: Data layer
serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1"
uuid = { version = "1", features = ["serde"] }
chrono = { version = "0.4", features = ["serde"] }
thiserror = "2"

# Phase 2: Templates
askama = "0.12"
clap = { version = "4", features = ["derive"] }
anyhow = "1"

content.text:
Here's my assessment, from looking at cct's source and a sweep of crates.io.

**The roundtrip diffs aren't actually a blocker.** Note the check_one numbers: 0 parse errors on all 485 lines. cct reads everything; the "RT diff" lines mean its typed structs silently *drop* fields they don't model when re-serializing (its `#[serde(flatten)]` catch-alls only cover some structs). That matters for cct's real purpose — lossless ingest into DuckDB — but a display command never re-serializes, so lossiness only hurts if one of the dropped fields is something we'd want to show (`promptSource`, skill names — mostly ancillary metadata). Still, the crate (`claude-code-transcripts` 0.1.11) hasn't been touched since 2026-05-05 and is shaped around ingest, not rendering.

**There are fresher crates.** The crates.io search turned up a healthy ecosystem; the standouts:

| crate | updated | what it is |
|---|---|---|
| `weavr` 1.2.0 | 2026-07-10 | Claude Code transcript → self-contained HTML/Markdown exporter; proper lib+bin crate, MIT |
| `chat-history` 0.2.0 | 2026-07-15 | search/inspect/export Claude Code, Cursor, Codex history |
| `obol-core` 0.8.0 | 2026-07-14 | reads Claude Code/Codex transcripts for cost estimation, library crate |
| `txcript` 0.3.0 | 2026-07-07 | converts transcripts between harness formats |

weavr is the closest fit to "display a .jsonl", and its parser is built exactly the way an evolving format demands: a custom `TranscriptEntry` deserializer where unknown `type` values land in an `Unknown { raw: Value }` variant, malformed lines become warnings instead of aborts, and common fields all default. So rendering never breaks when Anthropic adds a line type — new stuff just shows up untyped.

**But my recommendation is to own a small tolerant reader in vc-x1 rather than depend on any of them.** The reasoning:

- The format is undocumented and churns continuously (you've observed this directly), so *every* full-schema crate permanently trails. The winning posture is weavr's — parse each line as `serde_json::Value`, extract a small stable core, keep the raw value around — and that posture is ~200–300 lines to own, not a dependency's worth.
- The display core has been stable for a long time: `type`, `uuid`/`parentUuid`, `timestamp`, `message.role`, the content blocks (`text`, `thinking`, `tool_use`, `tool_result`). The churn is all in the ancillary metadata, which a viewer can ignore or dump raw behind a flag.
- Your actual goal — "see the change associated with a particular prompt" — is vc-x1-specific and no external crate touches it. That's a join between the transcript's uuid/timestamp thread and the bot repo's commit history plus ochid trailers. The viewer will want to grow toward that join (e.g. "show the transcript slice between bot-repo commit N and N+1"), which argues for owning the data model.
- weavr as a dependency would drag in askama (its HTML templating) unconditionally, and it's a one-person crate whose library API has no stability promise.

Where the clones do help: cct's `types.rs` and weavr's `src/model/` are both permissively licensed (MIT / MIT+Apache-2.0) field maps of the format — good reference material for naming and for which fields exist, without taking either as a dependency. weavr's `parser.rs` (BOM/blank-line tolerance, warnings-not-errors) is worth cribbing too. One more wrinkle to design for: the current session's file is being appended to live, so the reader should treat a truncated last line as normal, not an error.

The plain version: the library you tried isn't broken — it reads every line fine, it just forgets details it doesn't recognize when writing them back out, which a viewer never does. There are newer tools, and one (weavr) is genuinely well built. But because Anthropic keeps changing the file format, any outside library will always lag; the safe design is a small reader of our own that understands the handful of fields that never change and shrugs at the rest. That's also the only path to the real goal — linking a prompt to the commit it produced — which no outside tool knows anything about.

If that direction sounds right, the natural next step is a Todo entry for the viewer command (name TBD — `vc-x1 show-session`?) with the tolerant-core design sketched above.

# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

Intro paragraphs in `## Todo` and `## Bugs` should begin every
line with 1 leading space so they don't match the `^\d+\. `
pattern that locates numbered entries; 2 or 3 spaces also work.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run TODO.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](notes/todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](/AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. **Refactor: typed jj facade → jj-lib in-process; end
   subprocess spawning.** Version-control operations are
   ~30 hand-rolled `run("jj", …)` spawns plus every
   mutation, with per-module private wrappers and raw-git
   vestiges in init — stderr parsing instead of typed
   errors, and jj's single-attempt index-lock acquisition
   (the push `bookmark-set` lock race in [bugs.md](notes/bugs.md))
   can't be retried where it fails. A multi-ladder program;
   the staged plan, design detail, and the eight absorbed
   former Todos live in
   [refactor-20260716.md](notes/refactor-20260716.md).
   - Stages in execution order: DRY facade → hygiene
     riders → facade owns topology → de-gitify init →
     split push.rs → jj-lib migration → push body-intro
     validation → trapezoid close-out → por → dual
     conversion.
2. **vc-x1 push: pause point between commit and publish
   stages.** The merge non-ff close-out is a three-step
   sequence:
   - commit the close-out pair locally (normal 1:1 commit
     stages, ochids injected both directions)
   - rebase the code-side close-out into the merge (chids
     survive rebase, so every ochid stays valid)
   - publish

   Push has no supported stop after `commit-bot`, so today
   the recipe pre-commits both sides manually and resumes via

1	# Versioning
2	
3	How this project versions its commits and the running
4	artifact. The scheme — the cycle step-numbering and the
5	unique-per-commit aim — is generic and shared across projects:
6	this file is copied **verbatim**, with
7	[Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record)
8	covering each medium by conditional rather than per-project
9	edits.
10	
11	## Terms
12	
13	Three names, used as defined here across
14	[AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md),
15	[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md), and the notes files:
16	
17	- **version** — the per-commit version (e.g. `0.3.0-5.3.0`),
18	  written with `-` in prose, ladders, chores, todo, and commit
19	  talk; its suffix encodes the cycle phase (see
20	  [Step numbering](#step-numbering)).
21	- **version-of-record** — the authoritative stored copy of the
22	  version, in the project's manifest (see
23	  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record));
24	  a built or running artifact derives from it.
25	- **versioning** — the topic: this scheme as a whole.
26	
27	## Recording the version-of-record
28	
29	Where the version-of-record lives, how it's stored and
30	surfaced, and how often it changes — pick the case that fits
31	your medium:
32	
33	- **Manifest** — where the version-of-record is stored:
34	  - if Rust, `Cargo.toml` `[package].version`
35	  - if Python, `pyproject.toml` `[project].version` (or the
36	    committed config it's sourced from)
37	  - otherwise wherever the medium records it (a generic
38	    `version.toml`, a book's frontmatter, …) — add the case as
39	    needed
40	- **Notation** — how the `-` form is stored:
41	  - if the format allows `-` (TOML `version.toml`, `Cargo.toml`),
42	    store it verbatim
43	  - if it bars `-` (PEP 440's local segment, e.g. a Python
44	    project), remap to `+` — `0.3.0-5.3.0` → `0.3.0+5.3.0`: same
45	    version, just the stored spelling
46	- **Reporter** — how a built artifact surfaces the
47	  version-of-record:
48	  - if a CLI app, `<cli-app> -V`
49	  - if a TUI/GUI, add to Help/About or display on the title
50	- **Cadence** — how often to bump: see
51	  [Unique per commit](#unique-per-commit-preference-not-requirement);
52	  this project follows the per-commit preference.
53	
54	## Unique per commit (preference, not requirement)
55	
56	Our general notion is that the version-of-record should change
57	on **every commit**, so a built or running artifact identifies
58	the exact commit it came from.
59	
60	- This is a preference, **not** a hard requirement. A project
61	  following the cycle protocol may bump less often — once per
62	  cycle, only at release, and so on. Record the choice in
63	  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record)
64	  if it differs.
65	- It is achievable because the cycle's versions (below) are
66	  **pre-assignable**, unlike the git SHA, which a commit cannot
67	  contain (see the cycle protocol's
68	  [Commits backfill](cycle-protocol.md#commits-backfill)).
69	
70	## Step numbering
71	
72	The cycle (Preparation -> Work -> Close-out; see
73	[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md)) encodes each commit's
74	phase in the version suffix — the **final identifier `0` marks
75	a Preparation**:
76	
77	- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
78	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
79	- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
80	
81	**Preparation is optional.** A lightweight cycle — no ladder,
82	no setup commit — skips `-0` and starts at `-1` (its first
83	Work commit). The same holds at every level: a sub-cycle
84	needing no Preparation omits its `.0` (see Nesting). One that
85	grows a Preparation later adds the `0` step without
86	renumbering siblings.
87	
88	Disambiguation:
89	
90	- `-10` — Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
91	  Preparation.
92	- `-1.0` — Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
93	  identifier `0`).
94	
95	**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:
96	
97	- `X.Y.Z-3.0` — Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
98	- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` — its Work
99	- `X.Y.Z-3` — its Close-out
100	- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` — Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle
101	
102	Bump the version-of-record at the start of each phase, so the
103	active phase is recorded — and, per the preference above,
104	every commit carries a distinct version.
105

file.content:
# Versioning

How this project versions its commits and the running
artifact. The scheme — the cycle step-numbering and the
unique-per-commit aim — is generic and shared across projects:
this file is copied **verbatim**, with
[Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record)
covering each medium by conditional rather than per-project
edits.

## Terms

Three names, used as defined here across
[AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md),
[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md), and the notes files:

- **version** — the per-commit version (e.g. `0.3.0-5.3.0`),
  written with `-` in prose, ladders, chores, todo, and commit
  talk; its suffix encodes the cycle phase (see
  [Step numbering](#step-numbering)).
- **version-of-record** — the authoritative stored copy of the
  version, in the project's manifest (see
  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record));
  a built or running artifact derives from it.
- **versioning** — the topic: this scheme as a whole.

## Recording the version-of-record

Where the version-of-record lives, how it's stored and
surfaced, and how often it changes — pick the case that fits
your medium:

- **Manifest** — where the version-of-record is stored:
  - if Rust, `Cargo.toml` `[package].version`
  - if Python, `pyproject.toml` `[project].version` (or the
    committed config it's sourced from)
  - otherwise wherever the medium records it (a generic
    `version.toml`, a book's frontmatter, …) — add the case as
    needed
- **Notation** — how the `-` form is stored:
  - if the format allows `-` (TOML `version.toml`, `Cargo.toml`),
    store it verbatim
  - if it bars `-` (PEP 440's local segment, e.g. a Python
    project), remap to `+` — `0.3.0-5.3.0` → `0.3.0+5.3.0`: same
    version, just the stored spelling
- **Reporter** — how a built artifact surfaces the
  version-of-record:
  - if a CLI app, `<cli-app> -V`
  - if a TUI/GUI, add to Help/About or display on the title
- **Cadence** — how often to bump: see
  [Unique per commit](#unique-per-commit-preference-not-requirement);
  this project follows the per-commit preference.

## Unique per commit (preference, not requirement)

Our general notion is that the version-of-record should change
on **every commit**, so a built or running artifact identifies
the exact commit it came from.

- This is a preference, **not** a hard requirement. A project
  following the cycle protocol may bump less often — once per
  cycle, only at release, and so on. Record the choice in
  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record)
  if it differs.
- It is achievable because the cycle's versions (below) are
  **pre-assignable**, unlike the git SHA, which a commit cannot
  contain (see the cycle protocol's
  [Commits backfill](cycle-protocol.md#commits-backfill)).

## Step numbering

The cycle (Preparation -> Work -> Close-out; see
[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md)) encodes each commit's
phase in the version suffix — the **final identifier `0` marks
a Preparation**:

- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

**Preparation is optional.** A lightweight cycle — no ladder,
no setup commit — skips `-0` and starts at `-1` (its first
Work commit). The same holds at every level: a sub-cycle
needing no Preparation omits its `.0` (see Nesting). One that
grows a Preparation later adds the `0` step without
renumbering siblings.

Disambiguation:

- `-10` — Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
  Preparation.
- `-1.0` — Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
  identifier `0`).

**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:

- `X.Y.Z-3.0` — Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` — its Work
- `X.Y.Z-3` — its Close-out
- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` — Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle

Bump the version-of-record at the start of each phase, so the
active phase is recorded — and, per the preference above,
every commit carries a distinct version.

content.text:
I have everything needed for a comprehensive report.

---

# Architecture report: vc-x1 (for planning `show-session`)

## 1. Crate layout

**Single binary crate** (`name = "vc-x1"`, edition 2024, version 0.69.2). No workspace, no `crates/`. All source in `/home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/src/`. One binary (default, named from package). Key files:

- `src/main.rs` — entry point: clap tree assembly (`cli_with_banner`), the `Cli` parser struct, the `Commands` enum, `main()` dispatch match, and the `bm_track` bookmark-tracking diagnostic.
- `src/subcommand.rs` — the `SubcommandRunner` trait every subcommand implements; provides the uniform `dispatch(&ctx) -> ExitCode`.
- `src/context.rs` — `Context` struct (shared handle, currently just `user_config`), built once via `Context::load()`.
- `src/config.rs` — user-level config from `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml` (`UserConfig`, `AccountConfig`, `RepoSelector`); account/repo-target resolution for `init`.
- `src/toml_simple.rs` — hand-rolled minimal TOML loader (`toml_load` → `HashMap<String,String>`, `toml_get`); used for `.vc-config.toml`.
- `src/common.rs` — the utility hub: `run()` (subprocess), `mkdir_p`, `write_file`, `prompt`, `bold`/`bold_first_line`/`indent_body` (ANSI + formatting), commit formatters, `for_each_repo`, `find_workspace_root`, `default_scope`, `scope_to_repos`, `resolve_repos`, `CommonParams`. `src/common/tests.rs` holds its unit tests.
- `src/logging.rs` — `CliLogger` (custom `log::Log` impl; info→stdout, debug/trace→stderr, optional log file).
- `src/context.rs`, `src/repo_utils.rs` — repo lifecycle helpers (`prepare_local_repo`, `commit_initial`, `cross_ref_ochids`) wrapping `jj`/`git` via `common::run`.
- Subcommand modules (one file per command, each with `XxxArgs`/`XxxParams`): `chid.rs`, `desc.rs`, `list.rs`, `show.rs`, `validate_bot.rs`, `validate_desc.rs`, `fix_desc.rs`, `validate_todo.rs`, `fix_todo.rs`, `clone.rs`, `init.rs`, `symlink.rs`, `sync.rs`, `revert.rs`, `squash_push.rs`, `push.rs`.
- Support modules: `desc_helpers.rs`, `todo_helpers.rs`, `url.rs` (URL/target parsing incl. `derive_session_url`), `context.rs`.
- `src/options_flags/` — reusable clap arg leaves/bundles (`mod.rs` marker traits; `common_args.rs`, `scope.rs`, `repo.rs`, `account.rs`, `dry_run.rs`, `por.rs`, `private.rs`, `provision_bundle.rs`, `push_retry.rs`, `squash.rs`, `use_template.rs`, `config.rs`).
- Test-only: `src/test_helpers.rs`, `src/test_tmp_root.rs` (shared tempdir root, `#[path]`-included by integration tests too).

## 2. CLI structure

**clap derive**, single top-level `enum Commands` in `src/main.rs`. Pattern is a strict three-layer split (documented in ARCHITECTURE.md as "args → Context + Params"):

1. **`XxxArgs`** — `#[derive(clap::Args)]`, the CLI surface.
2. **`XxxParams`** — clap-free owned struct, built via `From`/`TryFrom<&XxxArgs>` (error type `String`).
3. **`fn xxx(ctx: &Context, params: &XxxParams) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>>`** — the op body.

Each `XxxArgs` implements `SubcommandRunner` (`src/subcommand.rs`) binding the three together:

```rust
impl SubcommandRunner for ChidArgs {
    type Params = ChidParams;
    fn to_params(&self) -> Result<Self::Params, String> { ChidParams::try_from(self) }
    fn run(ctx: &Context, params: &Self::Params) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
        chid(ctx, params)
    }
}
```

**Registration** — variant in the enum + one dispatch line in `main()`:

```rust
// src/main.rs, enum Commands
/// Check the bot repo is published ...
Chid(chid::ChidArgs),
...
// main() match
Commands::Chid(args) => args.dispatch(&ctx),
```

`dispatch` (the trait default) builds params (logging any `String` error via `error!` → `ExitCode::FAILURE`), brackets the run with `bm_track` enter/exit, runs, and maps `Ok(())`→`SUCCESS` / `Err`→`error!` + `FAILURE`.

**Errors:** no `anyhow`, no custom error enum. Ops return `Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>>`; param conversion returns `Result<_, String>`. Errors bubble to `dispatch` which logs them with `log::error!`. Ad-hoc errors are made with `format!("...").into()` or `.map_err(|e| format!(...))`.

Representative reader example — `validate_todo` (`src/validate_todo.rs`) is the closest analog to `show-session` (reads a plain file, no jj): `ValidateTodoArgs { file: PathBuf }` with `#[arg(value_name="FILE", default_value = TODO_FILE)]`, `From<&ValidateTodoArgs>` for params, body does `std::fs::read_to_string(&params.file).map_err(|e| format!("cannot read {}: {e}", ...))?`, emits via `info!`, and returns `Err(...)` to signal a non-zero exit.

## 3. Reusable utilities

- **`.vc-config.toml` loading:** `src/toml_simple.rs::toml_load(path) -> HashMap<String,String>` + `toml_get(&map, "workspace.other-repo")`. The file uses `[workspace] path = "/"`, `other-repo = ".claude"`.
- **User config:** `src/config.rs::load()` → `UserConfig`, reached via `ctx.user_config`.
- **Workspace/scope/bot resolution** (`src/common.rs`) — directly relevant to "read the .claude/ bot repo":
  - `find_workspace_root()` / `find_workspace_root_from(start)` — walk up to the dir whose `.vc-config.toml` has `path = "/"`.
  - `scope_to_repos(&Scope, workspace_root)` — `Side::Bot` → `root.join(other-repo)` (i.e. the `.claude/` path), with helpful errors when no bot repo is configured.
  - `resolve_repos(repo, scope)` — the composing `-R`/`-s` resolver used by chid/desc/list/show.
  - `Scope`/`Side` in `src/options_flags/scope.rs` (`ValueEnum` `code|bot`, `parse_scope`). `show-session` would likely take `--scope=bot` or resolve the bot path via `find_workspace_root().join(other-repo)`.
- **Shared commit-query args:** `src/options_flags/common_args.rs::CommonArgs` (flatten it if you want `-R`/`-s`/revision handling for free).
- **jj/subprocess invocation:** `src/common.rs::run(cmd, args, cwd) -> Result<String, Box<dyn Error>>` — the single subprocess helper; logs command at `debug!`, returns trimmed stdout, errors carry stderr. Used everywhere as `run("jj", &[...], dir)`. `bm_track_one` in main.rs shows the alternate direct `std::process::Command` pattern for capturing output.
- **Output/formatting:** everything goes through the `log` crate (`info!` for user output → stdout; `debug!`/`trace!` → stderr under `-v`). ANSI helpers in `common.rs`: `bold`, `bold_first_line`, `indent_body`. No table crate — formatting is manual with format strings and width params (see `show.rs`, `list.rs -w/--width`).
- **Logging setup / verbosity:** `src/logging.rs::CliLogger::init(verbose, log_path)`; global flags `-v`(count) and `--log <path>` are on `Cli` in main.rs (`global = true`). `-v`=debug, `-vv`=trace.
- **Path handling:** plain `std::path::{Path, PathBuf}`; `symlink.rs` has `~/.claude/projects` encoding logic (`default_symlink_dir`, cwd→`-home-user-project` encoding) if you ever need the Claude projects dir.

## 4. Dependencies (`Cargo.toml`)

```toml
chrono = { version = "0.4", default-features = false, features = ["now"] }
clap = { version = "4.6", features = ["derive", "wrap_help"] }
clap_complete = { version = "4.6", features = ["unstable-dynamic"] }
jj-lib = "0.41"
log = { version = "0.4", features = ["std"] }
pollster = "0.4"
# [target.'cfg(unix)'] libc = "0.2"
```

Notable for `show-session`: **there is no `serde`, no `serde_json`, and no TUI/terminal crate.** JSON/TOML are parsed by hand (`toml_simple`). Reading a `.jsonl` transcript would require either adding `serde`/`serde_json` (a new dependency — check house preference, the codebase currently avoids serde) or hand-parsing lines. `chrono` is present but only with the `now` feature and `default-features = false` (no parsing/formatting features enabled yet — you'd need to enable more features to format transcript timestamps). Color/bold is done via raw ANSI escapes, not a crate.

## 5. Test patterns

- **Unit tests:** in-module `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` (e.g. `scope.rs`, `symlink.rs`, `url.rs`), OR a sibling file wired with `#[path]`/`mod tests;` — e.g. `src/common/tests.rs`, `src/push/tests.rs`, `src/init/tests.rs`, `src/sync/tests.rs`. - **Subprocess integration tests:** `tests/` dir — `cli_smoke.rs`, `cli_init.rs`, `cli_keep.rs`, `cli_sync.rs`, sharing `tests/common/mod.rs`.
- **Fixture pattern** (`tests/common/mod.rs`): `CliFixture` = RAII tempdir under `$TMPDIR` with `HOME` overridden to an isolated dir so user config can't leak; `fx.cmd()` builds a `Command` for the binary via `CARGO_BIN_EXE_<pkg>`; `run_ok`/`run_err` assert exit status and embed stdout/stderr in panics. ## 6. Existing `.claude/*.jsonl` / session handling

**Nothing reads or parses Claude Code transcript `.jsonl` session files.** No occurrence of `jsonl` or `transcript` anywhere in `src/`. The word "session" throughout the codebase refers to the **bot repo** (the `.claude/` jj repo), not Claude Code chat transcripts:

- `src/url.rs::derive_session_url(code_url)` (line 147) — maps a code repo URL to its `.claude` counterpart (`foo.git` → `foo.claude.git`). Used by `clone.rs`, `init.rs`.
- `src/symlink.rs` — manages the `~/.claude/projects/<encoded-cwd>` symlink pointing at the workspace's `.claude` repo; `default_symlink_dir()` returns `~/.claude/projects`. This is the one place that touches the real Claude Code directory layout, useful for locating transcript files.
- `src/repo_utils.rs`, `push.rs`, `squash_push.rs`, `validate_bot.rs` — "session" = commits/writes in the `.claude` bot repo; `validate_bot.rs` defaults `-R` to `.claude` (`#[arg(short='R', long, default_value=".claude")]`).

So `show-session` would be genuinely new functionality: no existing JSONL parser to reuse, and you'll need to decide between adding `serde_json` vs. hand-parsing to stay consistent with the codebase's serde-free house style. The command would fit best as a new `src/show_session.rs` mirroring `validate_todo.rs`'s shape (a `PathBuf`/optional-path arg, `From`/`TryFrom` params, `info!`-based output), resolving the bot repo path via `common::find_workspace_root()` + `.vc-config.toml`'s `other-repo` (or `--scope=bot`), registered with one enum variant + one `dispatch` line in `main.rs`.

# Architecture report: vc-x1 (for planning `show-session`)

## 1. Crate layout

**Single binary crate** (`name = "vc-x1"`, edition 2024, version 0.69.2). No workspace, no `crates/`. All source in `/home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/src/`. One binary (default, named from package). Key files:

- `src/main.rs` — entry point: clap tree assembly (`cli_with_banner`), the `Cli` parser struct, the `Commands` enum, `main()` dispatch match, and the `bm_track` bookmark-tracking diagnostic.
- `src/subcommand.rs` — the `SubcommandRunner` trait every subcommand implements; provides the uniform `dispatch(&ctx) -> ExitCode`.
- `src/context.rs` — `Context` struct (shared handle, currently just `user_config`), built once via `Context::load()`.
- `src/config.rs` — user-level config from `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml` (`UserConfig`, `AccountConfig`, `RepoSelector`); account/repo-target resolution for `init`.
- `src/toml_simple.rs` — hand-rolled minimal TOML loader (`toml_load` → `HashMap<String,String>`, `toml_get`); used for `.vc-config.toml`.
- `src/common.rs` — the utility hub: `run()` (subprocess), `mkdir_p`, `write_file`, `prompt`, `bold`/`bold_first_line`/`indent_body` (ANSI + formatting), commit formatters, `for_each_repo`, `find_workspace_root`, `default_scope`, `scope_to_repos`, `resolve_repos`, `CommonParams`. `src/common/tests.rs` holds its unit tests.
- `src/logging.rs` — `CliLogger` (custom `log::Log` impl; info→stdout, debug/trace→stderr, optional log file).
- `src/context.rs`, `src/repo_utils.rs` — repo lifecycle helpers (`prepare_local_repo`, `commit_initial`, `cross_ref_ochids`) wrapping `jj`/`git` via `common::run`.
- Subcommand modules (one file per command, each with `XxxArgs`/`XxxParams`): `chid.rs`, `desc.rs`, `list.rs`, `show.rs`, `validate_bot.rs`, `validate_desc.rs`, `fix_desc.rs`, `validate_todo.rs`, `fix_todo.rs`, `clone.rs`, `init.rs`, `symlink.rs`, `sync.rs`, `revert.rs`, `squash_push.rs`, `push.rs`.
- Support modules: `desc_helpers.rs`, `todo_helpers.rs`, `url.rs` (URL/target parsing incl. `derive_session_url`), `context.rs`.
- `src/options_flags/` — reusable clap arg leaves/bundles (`mod.rs` marker traits; `common_args.rs`, `scope.rs`, `repo.rs`, `account.rs`, `dry_run.rs`, `por.rs`, `private.rs`, `provision_bundle.rs`, `push_retry.rs`, `squash.rs`, `use_template.rs`, `config.rs`).
- Test-only: `src/test_helpers.rs`, `src/test_tmp_root.rs` (shared tempdir root, `#[path]`-included by integration tests too).

## 2. CLI structure

**clap derive**, single top-level `enum Commands` in `src/main.rs`. Pattern is a strict three-layer split (documented in ARCHITECTURE.md as "args → Context + Params"):

1. **`XxxArgs`** — `#[derive(clap::Args)]`, the CLI surface.
2. **`XxxParams`** — clap-free owned struct, built via `From`/`TryFrom<&XxxArgs>` (error type `String`).
3. **`fn xxx(ctx: &Context, params: &XxxParams) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>>`** — the op body.

Each `XxxArgs` implements `SubcommandRunner` (`src/subcommand.rs`) binding the three together:

```rust
impl SubcommandRunner for ChidArgs {
    type Params = ChidParams;
    fn to_params(&self) -> Result<Self::Params, String> { ChidParams::try_from(self) }
    fn run(ctx: &Context, params: &Self::Params) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
        chid(ctx, params)
    }
}
```

**Registration** — variant in the enum + one dispatch line in `main()`:

```rust
// src/main.rs, enum Commands
/// Check the bot repo is published ...
Chid(chid::ChidArgs),
...
// main() match
Commands::Chid(args) => args.dispatch(&ctx),
```

`dispatch` (the trait default) builds params (logging any `String` error via `error!` → `ExitCode::FAILURE`), brackets the run with `bm_track` enter/exit, runs, and maps `Ok(())`→`SUCCESS` / `Err`→`error!` + `FAILURE`.

**Errors:** no `anyhow`, no custom error enum. Ops return `Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>>`; param conversion returns `Result<_, String>`. Errors bubble to `dispatch` which logs them with `log::error!`. Ad-hoc errors are made with `format!("...").into()` or `.map_err(|e| format!(...))`.

Representative reader example — `validate_todo` (`src/validate_todo.rs`) is the closest analog to `show-session` (reads a plain file, no jj): `ValidateTodoArgs { file: PathBuf }` with `#[arg(value_name="FILE", default_value = TODO_FILE)]`, `From<&ValidateTodoArgs>` for params, body does `std::fs::read_to_string(&params.file).map_err(|e| format!("cannot read {}: {e}", ...))?`, emits via `info!`, and returns `Err(...)` to signal a non-zero exit.

- **User config:** `src/config.rs::load()` → `UserConfig`, reached via `ctx.user_config`.

- **Shared commit-query args:** `src/options_flags/common_args.rs::CommonArgs` (flatten it if you want `-R`/`-s`/revision handling for free).

## 4. Dependencies (`Cargo.toml`)

```toml

## 5. Test patterns

- **Unit tests:** in-module `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` (e.g. `scope.rs`, `symlink.rs`, `url.rs`), OR a sibling file wired with `#[path]`/`mod tests;` — e.g. `src/common/tests.rs`, `src/push/tests.rs`, `src/init/tests.rs`, `src/sync/tests.rs`. content.content:
107	## Preparation
108	
109	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`), when the cycle needs
110	setup (a lightweight cycle omits it — see
111	[versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering)):
112	
113	- **Bump the version-of-record** to `X.Y.Z-0`. Where it
114	  lives, the suffix scheme, and any derived files (a
115	  lockfile, a sourced manifest version) are
116	  project-specific — see [versioning.md](versioning.md).
117	- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
118	  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
119	  - A **bold title line** — that will be the chores
120	    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
121	  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
122	  - A **plan ladder**.
123	- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
124	  append a `##` header with the title it records — the
125	  cycle's anticipated close-out title.
126	
127	## Work-N
128	
129	The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
130	implement the change. As many as needed:
131	
132	- Each commit runs through the
133	  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
134	- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
135	  visibility).
136	- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
137	  [Pushing](#pushing)).
138	- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
139	  scope grows enough (see
140	  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).
141	
142	## Close-out
143	
144	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
145	only — the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
146	what happens post-squash:
147	
148	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
149	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
150	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
151	  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
152	  placeholder opened during Preparation):
153	  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
154	    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the
155	    chores `##` header already carries that string).
156	  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
157	  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
158	    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
159	    back-reference.
160	  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
161	    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
162	  - Replace `## In Progress` with
163	    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
164	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
165	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
166	
167	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
168	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
169	see [Pushing](#pushing).
170	
171	## Numbering
172	
173	Each commit's phase is encoded in the version suffix — `-0`
174	Preparation, `-1`/`-2`/… Work, bare `X.Y.Z` Close-out,
175	recursively for sub-cycles. The full scheme — disambiguation,
176	nesting, optional Preparation, the project's version-of-record
177	format, and the per-phase bump — lives in
178	[versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering), which is the
179	single source of truth for this repo's versioning.
180	
181	## Per-commit flow
182	
183	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
184	through:
185	
186	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
187	   `TODO.md > ## In Progress` (`TODO.md` is at the repo
188	   root).
189	2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
190	   for the loop-and-squash technique).
191	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
192	   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit.
193	4. **Validate the artifact** — a medium-specific step, skip-able
194	   for notes-only commits, mandatory at close-out. For the Rust
195	   example the cargo cycle is:
196	   1. `cargo fmt`
197	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
198	   3. `cargo test`
199	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
200	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
201	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
202	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
203	   changes and we iterate until complete.
204	6. **Write the commit description** — see
205	   [Commit description](#commit-description).
206	7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
207	   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
208	8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
209	   the work repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
210	   keeps the verb visible):
211	
212	   ```
213	   jj commit -m \
214	   "<type>: <short description>" \
215	   -m "<intro paragraph>
216	
217	   - file1: gist
218	   - file2: gist
219	
220	   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
221	   -R .
222	   ```

file.content:
## Preparation

The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`), when the cycle needs
setup (a lightweight cycle omits it — see
[versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering)):

- **Bump the version-of-record** to `X.Y.Z-0`. Where it
  lives, the suffix scheme, and any derived files (a
  lockfile, a sourced manifest version) are
  project-specific — see [versioning.md](versioning.md).
- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
  - A **bold title line** — that will be the chores
    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
  - A **plan ladder**.
- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
  append a `##` header with the title it records — the
  cycle's anticipated close-out title.

## Work-N

The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
implement the change. As many as needed:

- Each commit runs through the
  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
  visibility).
- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
  [Pushing](#pushing)).
- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
  scope grows enough (see
  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).

## Close-out

The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
only — the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
what happens post-squash:

- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
  placeholder opened during Preparation):
  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the
    chores `##` header already carries that string).
  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
    back-reference.
  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
  - Replace `## In Progress` with
    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
see [Pushing](#pushing).

## Numbering

Each commit's phase is encoded in the version suffix — `-0`
Preparation, `-1`/`-2`/… Work, bare `X.Y.Z` Close-out,
recursively for sub-cycles. The full scheme — disambiguation,
nesting, optional Preparation, the project's version-of-record
format, and the per-phase bump — lives in
[versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering), which is the
single source of truth for this repo's versioning.

## Per-commit flow

Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
through:

1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
   `TODO.md > ## In Progress` (`TODO.md` is at the repo
   root).
2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
   for the loop-and-squash technique).
3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit.
4. **Validate the artifact** — a medium-specific step, skip-able
   for notes-only commits, mandatory at close-out. For the Rust
   example the cargo cycle is:
   1. `cargo fmt`
   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
   3. `cargo test`
   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
   changes and we iterate until complete.
6. **Write the commit description** — see
   [Commit description](#commit-description).
7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
   the work repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
   keeps the verb visible):

   ```
   jj commit -m \
   "<type>: <short description>" \
   -m "<intro paragraph>

   - file1: gist
   - file2: gist

   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
   -R .
   ```

**Two overrides apply:**

- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
  surface it; don't push through.
- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
  a review or question forward.

questions.question: How should show-session parse the JSONL? The codebase currently has no serde dependency (TOML is hand-rolled in toml_simple.rs), so this sets precedent.

options.label: serde_json, Value-only (Recommended)
options.description: Add serde_json; parse each line to serde_json::Value and extract the stable display core by path. No derive structs, so schema churn can't break parsing — unknown fields just ride along. Smallest robust option.

options.label: serde derive structs
options.description: Add serde + serde_json with typed structs like cct/weavr (Unknown variant + flatten extras). More type safety for rendering code, but more code to maintain as the format evolves.

options.label: Hand-rolled JSON parser
options.description: Zero new dependencies, matching toml_simple.rs style. A correct JSON parser (escapes, nesting, unicode) is far more work than the TOML subset was — high cost for dependency purity.

questions.question: What input should v1 of show-session accept?

options.label: File path only (Recommended)
options.description: Positional FILE arg, like check_one. Session discovery/listing (by id, by recency, tied to commits via ochid) comes in later cycles once the renderer exists.

options.label: Path, default = latest
options.description: FILE optional; when omitted, pick the most recently modified *.jsonl in the bot repo. Convenient but adds discovery logic to the first cycle.

options.label: Path or session id
options.description: Accept a path or a bare session id/uuid prefix resolved against .claude/*.jsonl. Most flexible, most v1 surface area.

questions.question: What should the default output look like?

options.label: Conversation view (Recommended)
options.description: Render the dialogue: user/assistant text in full, tool calls as one-liners (name + gist of input), thinking/meta/attachments hidden. Flags expand what's hidden (--tools, --meta, --thinking).

options.label: Index view
options.description: One line per entry: line number, type, role, timestamp, first-line preview. Good for orienting in a big file; a flag switches to full rendering.

options.label: Both from day one
options.description: Conversation view default plus an --index/--oneline mode in the same cycle. More surface in v1 but both views are cheap once parsing exists.

message.content:
I want rust structs with the data so I'm thinking something like parse the
json/jsonl to a map (or other object) that can handle any valid json/jsonl data
and then extract that information into our rust structs that we'll evolve over
time as we explore learn what's there and what is important for various
situations. Make sense?

content.text:
Makes complete sense — and it's arguably the best of both worlds. Two layers:

1. **Generic layer**: every line parses into a lossless any-JSON representation — this never breaks on schema churn, and the raw data stays available (for a `--raw` dump, or for spotting fields we don't model yet).
2. **Typed layer**: our own plain Rust structs (`SessionEntry`, content blocks, …) built by *hand-written extraction* from the generic layer — no serde derive on them, so we control exactly what's extracted, unknown/missing fields degrade gracefully instead of erroring, and the structs evolve as we learn what matters.

For the generic layer, `serde_json::Value` is precisely that "map that can handle any valid JSON" (`Object(Map<String, Value>)`, `Array`, `String`, `Number`, `Bool`, `Null`) — we'd use serde_json only as the parser producing Values, no `#[derive(Deserialize)]` anywhere. The alternative is hand-rolling a JSON parser to our own Value type in `toml_simple.rs` spirit, but full JSON (string escapes, unicode, nested arrays, number grammar) is a much bigger and riskier hand-roll than the TOML subset was; serde_json is the standard, battle-tested way to get that map.

So with that settled as the architecture, two questions remain:

questions.question: Is serde_json acceptable as the parser for the generic layer (Value-only, no derive), or should we hand-roll a JSON parser to keep zero new dependencies?

options.label: serde_json, Value-only (Recommended)
options.description: One new dependency used purely as a JSON-text → Value parser. Our structs stay serde-free; extraction is hand-written from Value.

options.label: Hand-rolled parser
options.description: Zero new deps, own Value-like enum in toml_simple.rs spirit. Full JSON grammar (escapes, unicode, numbers, nesting) is a substantial, correctness-sensitive hand-roll.

questions.question: What input should v1 of show-session accept?

options.label: File path only (Recommended)
options.description: Positional FILE arg, like check_one. Session discovery (by id, recency, or commit via ochid) comes in later cycles once parsing + rendering exist.

options.label: Path, default = latest
options.description: FILE optional; when omitted, pick the most recently modified *.jsonl in the bot repo. Convenient but adds discovery logic to the first cycle.

options.label: Path or session id
options.description: Accept a path or a bare session id/uuid prefix resolved against .claude/*.jsonl. Most flexible, most v1 surface area.

questions.question: What should the default output look like?

options.label: Conversation view (Recommended)
options.description: Render the dialogue: user/assistant text in full, tool calls as one-liners (name + gist of input), thinking/meta/attachments hidden. Flags expand what's hidden (--tools, --meta, --thinking).

options.label: Index view
options.description: One line per entry: line number, type, role, timestamp, first-line preview. Good for orienting in a big file; a flag switches to full text.

options.label: Both from day one
options.description: Conversation view default plus an --index/--oneline mode in the same cycle. More v1 surface, but both views are cheap once the typed layer exists.

input.description: Design show-session implementation
input.prompt:
Design a detailed implementation plan for a new `show-session` subcommand in the Rust CLI at /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1. Return the plan as your final text (do not write files).

## Purpose
Display a Claude Code transcript `.jsonl` file (session logs; this project keeps them in its `.claude/` bot repo, e.g. `.claude/4f5d498c-da5b-40ca-820e-a89676a58d79.jsonl`) as a readable conversation. Long-term goal (later cycles, not now): link prompts to the commits they produced, follow cross-file references (subagent sidechain files, compaction chains), an index view, session discovery.

## Agreed decisions (binding)
- Two-layer parse: serde_json used ONLY as a JSON-text → `serde_json::Value` parser (new dependency, Value API only, NO serde derive anywhere). Hand-written extraction from Value into our own plain Rust structs (the "typed layer") that we evolve over time. Unknown fields ride along in the Value; missing fields degrade gracefully.
- v1 input: positional FILE path only.
- v1 output: conversation view — user/assistant text in full, tool calls as one-liners (tool name + gist of input), thinking/meta/attachments/bookkeeping hidden by default, flags to reveal more.
- Command name: `show-session`.

## Codebase architecture (from exploration — follow these patterns)
- Single crate, clap 4 derive, edition 2024, version 0.69.2. Existing deps: chrono (default-features=false, features=["now"] — no parse/format features), clap, clap_complete, jj-lib, log, pollster. NO serde today.
- Subcommand pattern (see src/chid.rs, src/validate_todo.rs as models): `XxxArgs` (#[derive(clap::Args)]) → `XxxParams` (clap-free, via From/TryFrom<&Args>, error String) → `fn xxx(ctx: &Context, params: &XxxParams) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>>`. Each Args implements `SubcommandRunner` (src/subcommand.rs) with to_params/run; registered as a variant in `enum Commands` in src/main.rs plus one `Commands::Xxx(args) => args.dispatch(&ctx)` line.
- Errors: no anyhow; `Box<dyn Error>` + `format!(...).into()` / map_err with format!.
- Output: through `log` crate — `info!` → stdout for user output, debug!/trace! → stderr under -v. ANSI bold helpers in src/common.rs (`bold`, `bold_first_line`, `indent_body`). No table/TUI crates; manual format strings.
- Project lints: `unwrap_used = "warn"`, `expect_used = "warn"`; any unwrap/expect/unwrap_or* needs `// OK: reason` comment (and #[allow] for the panicking ones). Prefer shapes that avoid them.
- Doc comments mandatory: `//!` module header on every file, `///` on every fn/struct/field, intro + bullets style.
- Tests: in-module `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` or sibling file via `#[path]` (e.g. src/common/tests.rs); CLI integration tests in tests/ using tests/common/mod.rs `CliFixture` (tempdir, HOME override, fx.cmd() runs the binary, run_ok/run_err). validate_todo/fix_todo tests are the file-reading precedent.
- src/validate_todo.rs is the closest analog: `#[arg(value_name="FILE", ...)] file: PathBuf`, reads with fs::read_to_string mapped to a friendly error.

## Real data survey (from the project's .claude/*.jsonl, ~65k lines total)

## Codebase architecture (from exploration — follow these patterns)

- Subcommand pattern (see src/chid.rs, src/validate_todo.rs as models): `XxxArgs` (#[derive(clap::Args)]) → `XxxParams` (clap-free, via From/TryFrom<&Args>, error String) → `fn xxx(ctx: &Context, params: &XxxParams) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>>`. - Output: through `log` crate — `info!` → stdout for user output, debug!/trace! → stderr under -v. ANSI bold helpers in src/common.rs (`bold`, `bold_first_line`, `indent_body`). No table/TUI crates; manual format strings.

- Tests: in-module `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` or sibling file via `#[path]` (e.g. src/common/tests.rs); CLI integration tests in tests/ using tests/common/mod.rs `CliFixture` (tempdir, HOME override, fx.cmd() runs the binary, run_ok/run_err). validate_todo/fix_todo tests are the file-reading precedent.

input.content:
# Plan: `vc-x1 show-session` — display a Claude Code transcript

## Context

A project goal is seeing all bot session conversations and, eventually,
the change associated with a particular prompt. First step: a command
that displays one `.claude/<uuid>.jsonl` transcript as a readable
conversation.

Survey findings (this session): the cct crate parses today's files
without error but silently drops newer fields and lags the format;
weavr (crates.io) shows the right tolerant-parse posture. Decision:
own a small tolerant reader — the format is undocumented and churns
continuously, and the long-term goal (prompt ↔ commit linking via
ochids) is vc-x1-specific anyway.

**Agreed decisions (binding):**
- Two-layer parse: `serde_json` used **only** as text → `Value`
  parser (no derive anywhere); hand-written extraction from `Value`
  into our own plain Rust structs, evolved over time. Unknown fields
  ride along in the retained `Value`; missing fields degrade
  gracefully.
- v1 input: positional FILE path only. (Cross-file references —
  sidechain `agent-*.jsonl`, summary `leafUuid`, compaction chains —
  are real; design the typed layer so following them is additive.)
- v1 output: conversation view. Index view, discovery, and linking
  come in later cycles.
- Implementation runs at **medium effort** (per user); this plan was
  produced at xhigh.

## Data survey (grounding)

Across the project's `.claude/*.jsonl` (~65k lines): assistant 29120,
user 19087, then bookkeeping types (file-history-snapshot 4776,
permission-mode 3064, last-prompt 3016, system 2831, progress 2363,
ai-title 2249, attachment 2050, mode 699, queue-operation 131, …).
- assistant entries: one JSONL line **per content block**
  (tool_use / thinking / text); `message.id` identifies the API turn.
- user entries: `message.content` is a plain string (typed prompt,
  with `promptSource`) or an array of `tool_result` blocks.
- `isMeta` marks injected lines; `isSidechain` marks subagent lines;
  both `sessionId` and `session_id` appear.
- A live session's last line may be truncated — must tolerate.

## Module layout

### New: `src/transcript.rs` — parsing + typed layer (command-agnostic)

```rust
pub struct Transcript {
    pub entries: Vec<Entry>,
    pub malformed: Vec<(usize, String)>,   // (1-based line no, error)
}
pub struct Entry {
    pub line_no: usize,
    pub meta: EntryMeta,
    pub kind: EntryKind,
    pub raw: serde_json::Value,            // unknown fields ride along
}
pub struct EntryMeta {                     // all absent-tolerant
    pub uuid: Option<String>,
    pub parent_uuid: Option<String>,
    pub timestamp: Option<String>,         // raw ISO-8601, unparsed
    pub session_id: Option<String>,        // sessionId, then session_id
    pub is_sidechain: bool,
    pub is_meta: bool,
    pub cwd: Option<String>,
}
pub enum EntryKind {
    User { content: Vec<ContentBlock>, prompt_source: Option<String> },
    Assistant { content: Vec<ContentBlock>, message_id: Option<String> },
    System { subtype: Option<String> },
    Other { entry_type: String },          // every other/unknown type
}
pub enum ContentBlock {
    Text { text: String },
    Thinking { thinking: String },
    ToolUse { id: String, name: String, input: serde_json::Value },
    ToolResult { tool_use_id: String, text: String, is_error: bool },
    Other { block_type: String },
}
```

Functions: `parse_file(&Path) -> Result<Transcript, Box<dyn Error>>`
(errors only on I/O), `parse_str(&str) -> Transcript`, private
`parse_line`, and extraction helpers (`get_str`, `get_bool`,
`extract_content` — string content normalizes to one Text block —
`tool_result_text` handling both string and array-of-text shapes).
No panics: missing `type` → `Other`; truncated last line → one
`malformed` record. Tests in sibling `src/transcript/tests.rs`
(the `common/tests.rs` pattern).

### New: `src/show_session.rs` — command + renderer

`validate_todo.rs` shape: `ShowSessionArgs` (positional
`FILE: PathBuf`; flags `--thinking`, `--tools`, `--meta`) →
`ShowSessionParams { file, opts: RenderOptions }` via `From` →
`show_session(ctx, params)`; `SubcommandRunner` impl. Renderer as
pure private fns returning strings (unit-testable), printed via
`info!`: `render(&Transcript, &RenderOptions) -> (Vec<String>,
RenderStats)`, `tool_use_gist`, `short_time` (safe `.get(11..19)`
slice of the ISO string).

### Edits
- `src/main.rs`: `mod transcript;`, `mod show_session;`,
  `Commands::ShowSession(...)` variant + dispatch arm.
- `Cargo.toml`: `serde_json = "1"` (default features; pulls serde as
  a lib dep — accepted, no derive feature). No chrono change.

## Renderer (conversation view)

```
=== user 04:17:09 ===                 ← common::bold header
Let's do todo 1, move notes/todo.md ./TODO.md

=== assistant 04:17:15 ===
I'll start by reading the top of `notes/todo.md` ...
  [tool] Read: notes/todo.md (offset=0, limit=60)
```

- Header only when rendered role changes or assistant `message.id`
  changes — collapses one-line-per-block into visual turns.
- Default hides: thinking, tool results, meta/system/sidechain lines,
  all `Other` bookkeeping types. `--thinking` / `--tools` / `--meta`
  reveal (sidechain under `--meta` for v1).
- `tool_use_gist`: Bash → first line of `command`; Read/Write/Edit →
  `file_path`; fallback → compact `key=value` pairs, ~100 chars max.
- Tool results under `--tools`: indented, capped ~10 lines,
  `[result:error]` when `is_error`.
- No wrapping in v1; only one-liners truncate.
- Always end with a summary line: turns shown; hidden thinking /
  tool results / meta counts; skipped bookkeeping; malformed lines
  (omit zero clauses).

## Error handling

- Malformed line → `warn!` to stderr, recorded, continue; exit 0.
- Unknown types → skipped silently, counted in the summary.
- Only hard error: unreadable file (`cannot read {path}: {e}`).
- No `unwrap`/`expect`; project lint conventions apply
  (`// OK:` if one becomes unavoidable — alert in conversation).

## Tests

- `src/transcript/tests.rs` (inline JSON fixtures): typed prompt,
  each assistant block type, tool_result string + array shapes,
  `is_error`, `isMeta`/`isSidechain`, unknown type, missing `type`,
  blank line, truncated last line (count + line_no), `raw` retains
  unextracted fields.
- `show_session.rs` in-module tests: `tool_use_gist` (Bash multi-line,
  Read, unknown tool truncation), `short_time` edge cases, `render`
  on a synthetic Transcript (header collapsing, hide/reveal, stats).
- `tests/cli_show_session.rs` with `CliFixture`: ~10-line fixture
  covering the entry mix + a truncated final line; assert default
  output (prompt + assistant text + `[tool]`, no thinking/result
  bodies, one `warn:`), the three reveal flags, and `run_err` on a
  missing file.

## Cycle ladder (0.70.0 — confirm at Preparation)

Titles share the greppable stem `show-session`:

- `0.70.0-0` `chore: open show-session cycle` — version bump;
  In Progress block in TODO.md (problem + this ladder); open chores
  section in `notes/chores/chores-13.md` (or new -14 if 13 is full).
- `0.70.0-1` `feat: transcript parse + typed layer for show-session`
  — serde_json dep; `src/transcript.rs` + tests; `mod transcript;`.
  Keep clippy clean by having tests exercise the public surface
  (avoid blanket `#[allow(dead_code)]`).
- `0.70.0-2` `feat: show-session command + conversation renderer` —
  `src/show_session.rs`, main.rs wiring, integration tests.
- `0.70.0` close-out — bookkeeping per cycle protocol (Done entry,
  chores fill, notes/README.md update for the new subcommand);
  mandatory full cargo cycle; push per Pushing policy (user approval).

Per-commit flow throughout: mark `(current)`, work, flip `(done)`,
cargo fmt / clippy -D warnings / test / install, **stop for user work
review before describing**, description review, commit. Known risks:
header-collapsing may split oddly around sidechain lines (revisit in
the sidechain cycle); empty `thinking` blocks render nothing even
under `--thinking`.

## Verification

- Full cargo cycle green (fmt, clippy --all-targets -D warnings,
  test, install --path . --locked).
- End-to-end: `vc-x1 show-session .claude/4f5d498c-....jsonl` on the
  real transcript — readable conversation, sane summary line; again
  with `--thinking --tools --meta`; on the *current* live session
  file — truncated-line warning but exit 0.

content.content: File created successfully at: /home/wink/.claude/plans/declarative-orbiting-quokka.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)
